explanation_md: `Counter a` uses the default constructor which delegates to `Counter{0}`, setting `m_count` to 0. Two `tick()` calls bring it to 2. `Counter b{5}` initializes `m_count` to 5; one `tick()` call brings it to 6. Output is `2 6`.

options.a: Version A is syntactic sugar for Version B; both produce identical machine code.
options.b: Version A directly initializes `m_side`; Version B default-initializes it then assigns — and for `const` or reference members, Version B is ill-formed.
options.c: Version B is preferred because it is easier to read.
options.d: Version A requires `m_side` to be `const`; Version B does not.

explanation_md: Member initializer lists initialize the member directly during construction. Constructor-body assignment first default-initializes the member, then assigns a new value — two operations instead of one. More critically, `const` members and reference members have no default initialization and cannot be assigned after construction, making body assignment ill-formed for those cases.

options.a: `explicit` blocks the implicit conversion of `36.6` to `Temp`, so the call fails to compile — not a runtime bug.
options.b: The `val` member is `double`, so `36.6` is truncated to an `int` before conversion.
options.d: The copy constructor is missing, causing `val` to be zero.

explanation_md: `Temp(double)` is marked `explicit`, so passing the raw literal `36.6` to `implicit_ok(Temp t)` is an implicit conversion — which `explicit` forbids. This is a compile error, not a runtime bug that prints `false`. The student should call `implicit_ok(Temp{36.6})` explicitly.

options.a: This is undefined behavior because a constexpr constructor was given a non-constant argument.
options.b: `constexpr` on a constructor means it can be used at compile time if given constant arguments, but it still works as a normal constructor at runtime.
options.c: The program will only compile in debug mode, not release mode.
options.d: `constexpr` constructors are forbidden from accepting `int` parameters.

explanation_md: `constexpr` on a constructor enables compile-time use; it does not restrict runtime use. When called with runtime values, the constructor behaves exactly like a non-constexpr constructor. When called with constant expressions, the compiler can evaluate it at compile time. The two modes coexist transparently.

`make(7)` returns a prvalue `Val{7}`. In C++17, `Val v = make(7)` triggers mandatory copy elision — the prvalue is constructed directly into `v` without calling the copy constructor. No `copy ` is printed. Only `7` is printed.
